title: Ash-e reshteh
subtitle: Persian herb, bean, and noodle soup with kashk and fried mint.
excerpt: The most beloved of Persian herb soups. A thick green pot of chickpeas, lentils, kidney beans, and noodles, finished with fistfuls of fresh herbs and topped with caramelised onion, fried mint, and a swirl of kashk. Made for Nowruz and any cold-weather supper. An hour and a half on the hob; serves six.
sourceNotes:
Persian home cookery, particularly Iranian Tehran and the central plateau. Documented in Margaret Shaida's The Legendary Cuisine of Persia (1992) and in earlier Persian cookery manuscripts. Made for Nowruz, the Persian new year, where the soup signals new beginnings.

Reshteh are flat noodles roughly the shape of fettuccine. Persian shops sell them dried under the name reshteh polo; outside Iran, linguine, fettuccine, or broken-up tagliatelle stand in well.

Kashk is a fermented whey product traditionally drained from yoghurt or buttermilk and dried into hard balls. Liquid kashk is sold in Middle Eastern shops; sour cream or strained Greek yoghurt thinned with a little salt water substitutes well.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to two months. The noodles soften further on freezing, so leave them slightly al dente. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat slowly with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles for a crowd. A large stockpot is needed; the herbs alone fill a saucepan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Build the soup base the day before; chill, then reheat with the herbs added fresh and the noodles cooked in. The garnishes go on at the table.

recipeTools.notes: A 5-litre stockpot or large Dutch oven. The volume of herbs is high and the soup needs room.
For the onion and mint garnish, which fry separately.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fermented whey product traditional to Persian cookery, sold liquid in jars or as hard dried balls. The texture and tang of liquid kashk sit between sour cream and goat cheese. A 1:1 substitute is thick Greek yoghurt thinned with a tablespoon of water and a pinch of salt.

Persian flat noodles, shaped like fettuccine and sold dried in Middle Eastern shops. Reshteh noodles are made with a slightly higher salt content than Italian pasta, which holds them firmer in a long simmer. Linguine, fettuccine, or broken tagliatelle stand in well outside Iran.

content.text: Ash-e reshteh is the most loved of Persian soups, a thick green pot of beans, lentils, noodles, and a huge volume of herbs, finished with caramelised onion, fried mint, and a swirl of kashk. The soup is dense enough to spoon and rich enough to be a meal in itself; in Iran it is the centrepiece of Nowruz, the Persian new year, where the noodles signal new paths to take.
The base is the volume of herbs. Parsley, coriander, dill, chives, and spinach by the handful go into the pot near the end, turning the broth from beige to deep forest green. The noodles are
reshteh
, flat Persian noodles sold dried in Middle Eastern shops; linguine or fettuccine stand in. The garnish that makes the dish is liquid
kashk
, a fermented whey product with the tang of sour cream and the body of goat cheese.
An hour and a half on the hob, of which most is the beans simmering. Plan for the herb-stripping; it takes longer than the rest of the prep.
What you need

items.prepNote: dried, soaked overnight in cold water
finely chopped, or spring onion greens
linguine or fettuccine, broken into 5 cm lengths
thinned with 2 tbsp water and a pinch of salt as kashk substitute

content.text: Method
Cook the beans separately first

Drain the soaked {{chickpeas-dried}} of chickpeas and {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans. Put each into its own pan, cover with plenty of fresh cold water, and bring to a simmer. Cook chickpeas for forty-five minutes and kidney beans for an hour, until each is tender. Drain and set aside. The beans cook to different speeds; doing them in the same pot leaves one mushy and the other firm.
Build the soup base

Set the stockpot over a medium heat with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Add {{onion}} of finely chopped onion and cook for ten minutes until golden, stirring often. Stir in {{garlic}} of garlic and {{turmeric}} of turmeric and cook for another minute until fragrant.

Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock. Add {{lentils-green}} of rinsed green lentils and the cooked chickpeas and kidney beans. Bring to a simmer; cook for thirty minutes until the lentils are tender. The soup should still be loose; if it has thickened too far, top up with hot water.
Add the herbs in two passes

Add the chopped {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, {{coriander}} of coriander, {{dill}} of dill, {{chives}} of chives, and {{spinach}} of spinach. Stir everything in; the herbs settle into the broth and the colour turns green. Simmer for fifteen minutes, partly covered, stirring occasionally so the herbs do not catch at the bottom.
Drop in the noodles

Break {{pasta-dried}} of pasta into 5 cm lengths and add to the pot. Cook for eight to ten minutes, stirring, until the noodles are tender. The soup will have thickened to a stew consistency; loosen with hot water if you want it more spoonable. Taste; salt; pepper.
Fry the onion and mint garnish

Set a frying pan over a medium heat with 3 tbsp olive oil. Add {{onion}} of thinly sliced onion and fry for twelve to fifteen minutes, stirring often, until the slices turn deep gold and crisp at the edges. Lift half out onto a plate. Add {{mint}} of dried mint to the remaining onion in the pan, stir once, and pull off the heat immediately; mint burns to bitterness in seconds.
Plate, garnish, swirl

Thin {{greek-yoghurt}} of Greek yoghurt with two tablespoons of cold water and a pinch of salt to mimic kashk. Ladle the soup into deep bowls. Spoon over the fried onion, drizzle the mint-onion mixture, and finish with a thick swirl of the yoghurt. Squeeze a wedge of lemon over the top if you like the brightness. Serve with warm flatbread.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Ash is forgiving once the beans are done. The two failure modes are too thin or too thick.

items.cause: Cooked too long or noodles absorbed too much liquid.
items.fix: Top up with hot water or stock and stir through. Reshteh and pasta both swell as they sit; a sturdy soup at supper turns into a stew the next day. Loosen on reheating.

items.cause: Beans not cooked long enough to break down, or too much stock.
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for another fifteen minutes to reduce. A handful of the cooked beans crushed against the side of the pot with a wooden spoon helps thicken the broth.

items.symptom: Fried mint tastes burnt and bitter.
items.cause: Heat too high or pan too hot when the mint went in.
items.fix: Mint goes in off the heat or at the lowest setting, and only for ten seconds. The oil should still be hot from the onion; the mint colours rather than fries. Discard and start the mint over if it has gone black.

items.cause: Simmered too long with the lid on.
items.fix: Add the herbs in the last twenty minutes, partly covered; longer cooking with the lid sealed turns them army green. The soup is meant to be deep forest green, not olive.

content.text: Variations

Persian cooks vary the bean mix to taste. Black-eyed beans replace one of the standard varieties in some kitchens; navy beans or borlotti work too. A handful of split yellow peas in place of half the lentils adds body. The herb mix is more fixed; the standard quartet of parsley, coriander, dill, and chives is what makes the dish recognisable.

For a vegan version, swap the yoghurt swirl for a thick tahini paste thinned with lemon juice and a pinch of salt. The flavour profile shifts but the visual effect of the white swirl across the green soup is preserved.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Build the bean and herb base the day before, holding the noodles back. The next day, reheat the base, drop the pasta in fresh, and finish with the garnishes. The flavours improve overnight; the noodles do not.

Freezes well for up to two months in portioned containers. The pasta softens further on freezing; leave it slightly al dente on the first cook if you plan to freeze. Garnishes always go on fresh, never frozen with the soup.
Where this dish lives

Ash is the family of Persian thick soups, of which ash-e reshteh is the most widely known. The Nowruz association is the strongest tie; the noodles symbolise the many paths a new year offers, and the kashk swirl signals abundance. Iranian homes make this in vast batches for the celebrations and again through the colder months.

Margaret Shaida's The Legendary Cuisine of Persia gives the canonical English-language version. Iranian regional variants run from heavier meat-bone broths in the north to lighter pulse-and-herb pots in the central plateau; the dish travels well across the diaspora. A bowl of ash and warm sangak bread is the supper that Iranian winter kitchens settle into for months.
